criteria: Pool initializes with N blocks carved from a single contiguous memory allocation obtained via aligned_alloc (or malloc with manual alignment)
Block size is automatically rounded up to satisfy platform alignment requirements (minimum alignof(max_align_t)) and is at least sizeof(void*) to hold the free list pointer
pool_alloc() returns a pointer to a free block in O(1) by popping from the free list head; returns NULL when pool is exhausted (free_list_head == NULL)
pool_free(ptr) validates pointer bounds and alignment, then returns the block to the free list in O(1) by pushing to the free list head
Double-free is detected via bitmap state tracking; attempting to free an already-free block returns false with error message
Invalid pointer detection: pool_free() rejects pointers outside pool bounds or not aligned to block boundaries
All returned pointers are aligned to the enforced alignment boundary; verified by assertion ((uintptr_t)ptr % alignment == 0)
pool_destroy() frees the base allocation and bitmap; logs warning if blocks remain allocated
Statistics API provides total capacity, allocated count, and free count
Benchmark demonstrates 1 million alloc/free cycles complete in under 50ms (target: ~8-10ns per operation)
pool_alloc() automatically allocates a new chunk of N blocks when the free list is empty, where N equals the initial block count
All allocated chunks are tracked in a singly-linked Chunk list with metadata stored separately from block memory
pool_free() correctly handles blocks from any chunk without requiring chunk identification from the caller
find_block_index() performs O(chunks) search to validate pointers and detect double-frees across all chunks
Configurable max_chunks limit causes pool_alloc() to return NULL instead of growing when the limit is reached
Configurable max_bytes limit prevents growth that would exceed the total memory ceiling
pool_destroy() frees all chunk memory and chunk metadata, preventing all memory leaks
pool_destroy() logs a warning message with the count of still-allocated blocks when leaks are detected
PoolStats API reports: total_blocks, allocated, free, chunk_count, block_size, total_bytes, and overhead_bytes
Bitmap automatically expands via realloc() when new chunks are added to cover the increased block count
Cross-chunk allocation test demonstrates blocks from chunk 2 can be freed and reallocated without corruption
Pool initializes with N blocks carved from a single contiguous memory allocation obtained via aligned_alloc with alignof(max_align_t) alignment
Block size is automatically rounded up to satisfy platform alignment (minimum alignof(max_align_t), typically 8 or 16 bytes) and is at least sizeof(void*) to hold the free list pointer
pool_alloc() returns a pointer to a free block in O(1) by popping from the free list head; returns NULL when pool is exhausted (free_list_head == NULL)
pool_free(ptr) returns the block to the free list in O(1) by pushing to the free list head
Double-free is detected by checking bitmap state (bitmap_test returns false for already-free block); attempting double-free prints error to stderr and returns false
All returned pointers are aligned to alignof(max_align_t); verified by asserting ((uintptr_t)ptr % alignof(max_align_t)) == 0
Benchmark: 1 million alloc/free cycles complete in under 50ms
pool_destroy() frees base memory and bitmap, prints warning if allocated > 0, and zeroes the pool structure
Bounds checking in pool_free rejects pointers outside pool range (before base or after base + capacity * block_size)
Alignment checking in pool_free rejects pointers not at block boundaries (offset % block_size != 0)
